coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-Ag nanoparticle membrane for preventing emerging contaminants in oil produced water
coredata.dc:creator: Zahmatkesh, Sasan
Ni, Bing-Jie
Klemeš, Jiří Jaromír
Bokhari, Awais
Hajiaghaei-Keshteli, Mostafa
coredata.dc:description: A polycarbonate copolymer was used to create a novel low-cost microfiltration membrane. Due to their antibacterial properties, carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles (CD-Ag NPs) have been synthesized and incorporated into a poly(acrylonitrile-styrene) membrane by the Creighton method. This work examined the membranes using Infrared Vibrational Spectroscopy, Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy, and a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). An investigation was conducted to determine the effects of the CDs-Ag NPs amount on the matrix's morphology, pore size, porosity, permeability, and mechanical strength. Also, photobleaching was used to reduce and stabilize CDs-Ag NPs, as reflected by a red shift in the spectra for CDs compared to CDs-Ag NPs from 290 nm to 570 nm in the UV/Vis spectrum, indicating the nanoparticle was generated. Pore size and mechanical strength are reduced when CDs-Ag NPs are added to neat membranes. However, it is followed by a decrease in porosity and mechanical strength. The optimized membrane exhibited a 0.66 pore size. In addition to removing oils and metals from wastewater, they also remove dyes, antibiotics, and other organic and inorganic compounds. The colony forming unit (CFU) test also showed that the percentage of CFU decreased as AgNO3 concentration increased so that at a concentration of 4, the percentage of CFU for E. coli and S. aureus was 5 % and 1.9 % for S. aureus. The Creighton methodology was therefore proven suitable for functionalizing the membrane, and discs-Ag NPs membranes have been demonstrated to be promising wastewater treatment membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Membrane
Emerging contaminants
Nanoparticle
Microfiltration
Wastewater treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Polycarbonate membrane incorporating carbon quantum dots and Ag nanocomposite synthesized by the Creighton method
Characterization and description of the C-dots (CDs)
Effect of CDs-Ag NPs on oil produce water
The surface structure of membranes on oil produce water

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Czech Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research has been supported by the project Sustainable Process Integration Laboratory – SPIL , funded as project No. CZ.02.1.01/0.0/0.0/15_003/0000456 , the Operational Programme Research, Development and Education of the Czech Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports by EU European Structural and Investment Funds, Operational Programme Research, Development and Education .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman spectrum of the colloidal CDs-Ag NPs (b) UV/VIS absorption spectrum of the colloidal CDs-Ag NPs.
The SEM image of (a) the neat membrane surface and (b) the AgNPs incorporated SAN Membrane and (c) the tensile strength of the SAN membrane using different amounts of AgNO3 on each specimen.

Spectrums of UV-VIS and FTIR absorption for C-dots, HR-TEM images of C-dots with green fluorescence, and HR-TEM images of C-dots with red fluorescence. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
After exposure to E. coli and S. aureus cells for 14 h at 38°, the antimicrobial properties of fabricated membranes using different amounts of AgNO3 were measured.
The permeate flux in different CDs-Ag NPs.
The permeate flux (L/m2.h) at different feed pressures.
Pore size distributions of polycarbonate/CDs-Ag NPs membranes with: 0 CDs-Ag nanocomposites; 1–1/5 CDs-Ag NPs, 2–2.5 CDs-Ag NPs, and 3,4 CDs-Ag NPs.
(a) Average size of oil particles (b) The comparison of the removal values at different pH values using different concentrations of CDS-Ag NPs.
(a) Equilibrium time in surface absorption process at pH = 7 and T = 22 °C, (b) The temperature effects on the adsorption of the oily wastewater on the synthesized CDS-Ag NPs.
The SEM images of biogenic CDs-Ag NPs at 100 nm and the membrane surface.
The thickness, pore size, and porosity of the fabricated membranes.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-Ag nanoparticle membrane for preventing emerging contaminants in oil produced water

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater
School of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater, School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Technology Sydney, Sydney, NSW 2007, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Tecnologico de Monterrey, Escuela de Ingenieríay Ciencias, Puebla, Mexico.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A polycarbonate copolymer was used to create a novel low-cost microfiltration membrane. Due to their antibacterial properties, carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles (CD-Ag NPs) have been synthesized and incorporated into a poly(acrylonitrile-styrene) membrane by the Creighton method. This work examined the membranes using Infrared Vibrational Spectroscopy, Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy, and a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). An investigation was conducted to determine the effects of the CDs-Ag NPs amount on the matrix's morphology, pore size, porosity, permeability, and mechanical strength. Also, photobleaching was used to reduce and stabilize CDs-Ag NPs, as reflected by a red shift in the spectra for CDs compared to CDs-Ag NPs from 290 nm to 570 nm in the UV/Vis spectrum, indicating the nanoparticle was generated. Pore size and mechanical strength are reduced when CDs-Ag NPs are added to neat membranes. However, it is followed by a decrease in porosity and mechanical strength. The optimized membrane exhibited a 0.66 pore size. In addition to removing oils and metals from wastewater, they also remove dyes, antibiotics, and other organic and inorganic compounds. The colony forming unit (CFU) test also showed that the percentage of CFU decreased as AgNO3 concentration increased so that at a concentration of 4, the percentage of CFU for E. coli and S. aureus was 5 % and 1.9 % for S. aureus. The Creighton methodology was therefore proven suitable for functionalizing the membrane, and discs-Ag NPs membranes have been demonstrated to be promising wastewater treatment membranes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water and wastewater treatment can benefit significantly from membrane technology [34]. Water and wastewater are commonly treated with membranes to remove pollutants [18]. Currently, polymer membranes [36] are prone to fouling, their most significant limitation [10]. A significant issue for membrane technology applications is membrane fouling, especially biofouling [15]. Water permeability can be significantly decreased by biofouling on the membrane surface. Pretreatment of feed, membrane cleaning, and modification of membrane surfaces are the methods used to reduce biofouling [3].

Recent advances in nanotechnology have heavily focused on designing and functionalizing plasmonic nanoparticles (NPs) [34]. The physical and chemical characteristics of plasmonic NPs smaller than 110 nm differ from those of their bulk counterparts [24]. In addition to their large surface/volume ratio, surface plasmon resonance modes, electrical characteristics, and antibacterial qualities, these materials exhibit many other characteristics [20]. Nanophotonic and optoelectronic devices, antimicrobial coatings and drug delivery, and water treatment are some of the industries that utilize plasmonic NPs. Ag was selected for the present study due to its antimicrobial and catalytic properties [1]. The peculiar optical characteristics of silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs), including the formation of surface plasmon resonances (SPRs) in the ultraviolet and visible spectrum [23], have also been studied for applications in sensing appliances. Localized SPRs arise due to collective oscillations of CB electrons on Ag NP surfaces during the electricity generated by the incoming light [25]. The production of Ag NPs can be accomplished orally [12], chemically [38], photochemically [26], electrochemically [11], or biologically [21]. Many distinct advantages are associated with using Ag NPs over conventional superoxide dismutase immobilization techniques, such as ease of synthesis, sensitivity, selectivity, and chemical and thermal stability [7]. In addition to their excellent non-enzymatic catalytic activity, they also offer many distinct benefits. Even though opto-chemical approaches using Ag NPs can detect O2•* within large linear ranges, with high selectivity and high stability, such technologies have significant shortcomings [13]. The preparation of quantum dots (CDs) has several significant limitations. Among these limitations are the ability to improve the surface area and the active sites by in situ functionalization during the preparation process. This can also be used to improve light confinement by minimizing the use of toxic substances [4].

There has been much interest in carbon CDs due to their high porosity, non-toxic nature, high biocompatibility, optical properties, and fluorescence signals [2]. New types of functionalized nanomaterials have emerged from them [29]. A CD and AgNP hybrid composition is achieved thanks to the surface reducing and hydrophilic functional groups of carbon-based dots [9]. By etching with an oxidant, medium-sized AgNPs of 5 nm suppressed CDs' fluorescence [17]. By combining a single-step hydrothermal blend strategy and high fluorescence emission, Liang et al., 2020. As of 2020, core-shell CD-AgNCs will be in production [16].

Furthermore, many hazardous organic compounds and heavy metal ions are found in soil and water worldwide due to their numerous health risks [19]. In addition, identifying, monitoring, and quantifying heavy metal ions and harmful organic compounds is necessary to develop a reliable and sensitive sensor system [37]. Even though these techniques are susceptible and reliable, performing them requires highly qualified and educated personnel [8]. Furthermore, they are unsuitable for detecting analytes onsite due to the requirement for a cumbersome pretreatment process [6]. This study uses a simple and expedient method for preparing CDs-Ag NPs at room temperature.

Hydrothermally reducing and stabilizing CDs made from peanut shells make it possible to assemble CDs-Ag NPs for the first time. Through various methods, it is possible to compare CDs-Ag NPs and CDs on optical, morphological, and structural grounds. CDs-Ag NPs synthesized catalyze the transformation of 4-nitrophenol into 4-aminophenol [5]. NPs and heavy metal ions possess a unique interaction that can be used to detect several compounds [14]. A study was also conducted to assess the antimicrobial properties of the synthesized nanoparticle.

This paper presents an analysis of the properties of polymer membranes, including polycarbonate, in addition to the synthesis of CDs-AgNPs. This analysis is done in the presence of synthesized CDs-AgNPs. Finally, antibacterial activity tests and membrane biofouling experiments have been performed to evaluate the performance of membranes used in industrial wastewater treatment. Based on the measurements of permeate flux and oil content in the permeate, the performance of the membranes was investigated.

In Tehran, Iran, KP Plastic manufactured the membranes using polycarbonate polymer grade 0710. In addition to silver nitrate, Merck also provides ethanol (EtOH, 99.9 % purity), and tetrahydrofuran (THF). Furthermore, nonsolvent additives in cast solution formulations include NMP, cellulose acetate, and glycero [34].

By using hydrothermal processes, CDs are most efficiently synthesized, and their luminescent properties are extraordinary. A hot air oven is used to heat the autoclave to 188 °C for 9 h. This is after securing the outer shell of the peanut in the autoclave. After grinding, DI water is added to the product. Following the ultrasonic bath, the mixture is centrifuged to obtain a well-dispersed solution. A UV illuminator reveals that the CDs in the supernatant are green in color.

Approximately 4 to 12 M of AgNO3 are prepared as a solution. Each CD and AgNO3 solution is applied in equal quantities. The beaker is filled with CD solution and stirred gently. To bind the Ag NPs on the outer side of the CDs, the AgNO3 solution is added dropwise to the CD solution. The reddish-brown coloration of the solution indicates that AgNO3 has been wholly reduced and a nanoparticle has been formed.

Various strains of gram-negative bacteria, such as E. coli, were used to investigate the antimicrobial activity of CD/Ag composites. Cultured E. coli plates were treated with the CDs/Ag composite at varying concentrations to study their antibacterial properties. CDs-Ag NPs were added in varying amounts in wells 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, and 4 agar surfaces labeled 1, 2, 3, and 4. CDs-Ag NPs demonstrated effective antimicrobial activity in all wells filled with the composites.

In order to analyze the antibacterial activity of the membrane, gram-negative E. coli and gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus were used as model bacteria. A shaker was used to incubate the membrane sheets at 70 °C for 12 h after they were immersed in the cell suspension. The viable cells in the suspension were then quantified by the colony-forming unit (CFU) method [33].

THF was used to dissolve polycarbonate and stir it for 12 h at 70 °C to fabricate the polycarbonate membrane. Following the casting, a deionized water bath was used to immerse the glass plate in the solution. For 50 h, the membrane was soaked in water to remove all solvents. In order to obtain a homogeneous, straightforward solution, 70 °C was selected for overnight storage and degassing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polycarbonate membrane incorporating carbon quantum dots and Ag nanocomposite synthesized by the Creighton method
ce:section.ce:para: Using the modified Creighton method, CDs-Ag NPs were synthesized by reducing them with NaBH4. The dropwise addition of CDs-Ag NPs to NaBH4 was carried out in an ice bath at 0 °C. Before UV–visible absorption spectrophotometry and micro-Raman spectroscopy characterization of the final colloid, it was refrigerated for at least four days. In this experiment, CDs-Ag NPs samples were scanned using the 2150-UV Spectrophotometer (UNICO, USA) within the 200–800 nm spectral range at a scanning interval of 1 nm. Spectroscopy (Avantes, Netherlands) was also used to verify Creighton CDs-Ag NPs.

A reaction with CDs-Ag NPs was performed in an ice bath at 0 °C to integrate AgNPs into polycarbonate membranes. Polycarbonate membranes were treated with a NaBH4 solution. The CDs-Ag NPs were reduced completely by stirring the colloidal solution for 1 h. Through micro-Raman spectroscopy, Fig. 1 illustrates the purity of the Creighton colloid. In Fig. 1 (a), H2O's bending and stretching vibrational modes show two Raman peaks. This results in the conclusion that CDs-Ag NPs are contaminant-free. In Fig. 1 (b), CDs-Ag NPs are shown with their UV–Vis absorption spectra.

A cross-flow filtration plant was used to conduct the dynamic biofouling experiment. At a pH of 7.1 and a temperature of 23 °C, the feed containing E. coli and CH3COONa was prepared. A deionized water wash was conducted on the module to prepare it for filtration. Next, a membrane was fitted to the module, and the filtration process was started, followed by a 24-h observation of the permeate flux. In order to measure the permeate flux after biofouling, the membrane was carefully washed twice [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and description of the C-dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 2 (a), CDs-Ag NPs are incorporated into the polycarbonate membrane and can be seen in the SEM image of the neat membrane surface. Fig. 2(b) shows that CDs-Ag NPs are located both within and on top of the membrane.

The porosity of membranes was determined by immersing membrane samples with a known weight for 50 h in isopropanol. After cleaning the water surfaces of the soaked membranes, the weight of the membranes was determined. Following the evaporation of the isopropanol from the samples, the samples were dried at 94 °C to ensure complete evaporation. The weights of each of the individuals were then measured. Eq. (1) can calculate each sample's porosity after the experiment has been performed three times for each sample.
(1)
ε =
m wet
−
m dry
ρAL
where m
dry and m
wet are the dry and wet membrane samples' weight in kg, respectively. L, A, and ρ are the sample thickness (m), sample area (m2), and pure liquid density (kg.m−3), respectively.

Table 1 shows that the membranes were fabricated with different pore sizes and porosities. According to Table 1, the porosity of the membrane increases as the pore size increases. Porosity and permeability can both be improved by CDs-Ag NPs. The addition of more NPs increases porosity and permeability, even though it increases the number of NPs. The CDs-Ag NPs are also found to enhance the bulk density and mechanical strength. Additionally, Table 1 provides measurements of the thickness of the membranes. Similar to pore size and porosity, membrane thickness followed a similar pattern.

Based on different amounts of AgNO3, Fig. 2(c) shows the tensile strength of the membranes. In general, the mechanical strength of CDs-Ag NPs is improved as the number of NPs increases. However, if the number of NPs is increased beyond what is considered optimal, the strength is decreased.

When AgNO3 was increased from 1 to 2, the specimen tensile strength increased from 125 kPa to 344 kPa. Furthermore, the tensile strength of the membranes decreased from 240 Kpa to 203 Kpa when AgNO3 concentrations were raised to 4. As the tensile strength of a membrane increases, membrane braiding can be defined. In terms of interaction with AgNO3, the structure exhibits more significant interaction until 2; after that, a reverse process occurs.

As shown in Fig. 3 , synthesized CDs exhibit an absorption spectrum. An absorption peak is visible at 277 nm, which is attributed to the transition between CC and CN bonds. Fig. 3 shows a photo of the synthesized CDs, which exhibit green fluorescence when exposed to UV, which is caused by surface defects.

An FTIR spectrum of the CDs was used to determine if they contained functionalities. CC, CO, and an epoxy ring are the bands observed in the spectrum at 3819, 3732, 3544, 3096, 2830, 2344, 1700, 1378, 1033, and 695 cm−1; these functionalities explain the water-soluble properties of the synthesized CDs.

E. coli and S. aureus were used to evaluate the antimicrobial properties of the CDs-Ag NPs. Fig. 4 (a) shows the diameters of the inhibition zones caused by CDs-Ag NPs solutions when diluted to various concentrations. Fig. 4 (b) shows that their inhibition zones can identify E. coli and S. aureus. Based on their antibacterial potential, composite volume was found to increase linearly with the increasing volume of composite suspension in culture dishes. This resulted in a linear increase in the zone of clearance. Thus, based on the antimicrobial screening assessment, the CDs-Ag NPs were found to possess effective antimicrobial properties against E coli and S aureus.

According to the results, the percentage of CFU decreases with an increase in the AgNO3 amount. As a result, E. coli CFU% reaches 8 %, and S. aureus CFU% reaches 4 %, respectively, at a concentration of 4. Furthermore, higher-porosity membranes removed more bacteria than low-porosity membranes.

Fig. 5 shows the permeate flux of fabricated membranes with different concentrations of CDs-Ag NPs in the filtration process. As shown in Fig. 6 , increasing the amount of CDs-Ag NPs initially decreases the membrane's permeate flux; however, increasing the amount of AgNPs above the optimal amount causes an increase in the permeate flux. It should be noted that the permeate flux is 450 L/M2.h using 1 g CDs-Ag NPs. At the optimum point (CDs-Ag nanocomposite s concentration = 2) the flux is reduced to 390 L/M2.h, and at CDs-Ag NPs concentration of 4, the permeate flux is reached at 511 L/M2.h.

Fig. 6 displays the results of an investigation into the effects of pressure and feed flow rate on the permeate flow. Increasing the pressure can improve the permeability, and increasing the feed flow rate can also enhance the permeate flux. All experiments had a biological oxygen demand (BOD) greater than 99.9 % for the permeate fluxes [28].
Using a cross-flow filtration plant containing E. coli suspension, the anti-biofouling behavior of the membrane was investigated [32].

Constant pressure filtering was conducted to determine whether the membranes have anti-fouling properties and the behavior of flux reduction. This method measured permeate flux for an hour using pure water as the feed for 58 min. A 45-min flow measurement was conducted with wastewater as feed [31]. The membrane was removed from the module and gently washed for 30 min to avoid fouling again after being cleaned with pure water. After the membrane for 58 min, wastewater was used as a feed to determine the amount of permeate flux. The same procedure was repeated in subsequent cycles. The fresh feed solution is replaced with wastewater at the beginning of each process cycle [30].

The cycle's filtration process was modeled based on the flux of water passing through the membrane with a CDs-Ag NPs amount of 2. Each cycle resulted in a significant decrease in purified water flux. In this case, the membrane may have become fouled within. Due to the strong attachment of biofouling to the surface of the membrane or within the pores, the decrease in flux, in this case, can be attributed to irreversible fouling [27].

The top surfaces of polycarbonate and polycarbonate/CDs-Ag NPs membranes are shown in Fig. 7 , illustrating the effects of CDs-Ag NPs on pore size distribution. The cumulative distribution percentage of pore sizes shows a shift to the right compared to the neat polycarbonate membrane. Moreover, the amount of CDs-Ag NPs added to the polycarbonate casting solution significantly affects pore size distribution. By adding 2.5–5 wt% of CDs-Ag NPs to the polycarbonate casting solution, extensive pore sizes resulted, with an expected accumulative distribution percentage of pore sizes ranging from 10 nm to 980 nm. The Polycarbonate/CDs-Ag NPs casting solution does not report the effect of the CDs-Ag NPs amount on pore size and cumulative percentage of pore size. Fig. 7. shows that the pore size distribution narrows when the amount of CDs-Ag NPs is increased to 2–3 wt% in the casting solution. As a result of the use of 1–1/5 wt% of Ag nanocomposite, the pore size distribution was narrow, with most pore sizes falling between 290 and 570 nm; the best pore size distribution was recorded with 1–1/5 wt% of AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs-Ag NPs on oil produce water

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 (a) shows that synthetic wastewater contains oil particles with an average size of 103.5 nm. The effect of pH value on municipal wastewater concentration was investigated in fixed amounts. The synthetic effluent was initially measured to have a COD of 250 mg/L. According to previous studies, the pH value range was chosen based on its optimum performance. According to Fig. 8(b), the rejection values at different CDs-Ag NP concentrations can be compared [35].

Water-oil emulsion COD reduction rate was investigated using absorption tests at a constant temperature of 22 °C for contact up to 250 mins and 25 min intervals. Furthermore, Fig. 9 (a) shows that surface absorption in constant operating conditions occurs in about 60 min, depending on how much oil is present. CDS-Ag NPs at this concentration will rapidly become saturated, preventing high removal efficiencies [39].

The absorption capacity is calculated as Eq. (2), which V is the volume of the solution and m is the adsorbent mass; and C0 and Ce are the initial and the final concentration, respectively. However, Fig. 9 (b) illustrates the effects of temperature on the adsorption of oily wastewater on the synthesized CDS-Ag NPs. All the results presented in Fig. 9 (b) indicate a decrease in adsorption with an increase in temperature since the adsorption process is an exothermic reaction.
(2)
q =
C 0 − Ce
× V
m

ce:section.ce:section-title: The surface structure of membranes on oil produce water
ce:section.ce:para: PC membrane surface pores have increased and their distribution has become more regular as a result of adding NPs to the polymer solution. Due to the greater compatibility that occurs in the polymer solution when modified NPs are added to the membrane structure, more regular and better surface cavities can be formed, resulting in a membrane that is more hydrophilic and has better properties.

Fig. 10 depicts membrane cross-sections of PC-CDs-Ag NPs (1.5 % wt), PC-CDs-Ag NPs (2.5 % wt), and PC-CDs-Ag NPs (3 % wt). As seen in the figure, NPs increase membrane porosity. There are two layers of membranes, one dense and one porous. Due to the lack of porosity on the surface of pure PSF membranes, greater amounts of water cannot penetrate to the sublayer, thereby forming large porous structures [38].

A CDs-Ag NPs were incorporated onto a polycarbonate copolymer membrane to produce a novel low-cost microfiltration membrane. Moreover, Creighton was used to synthesize the CDs-Ag NPs in this way. Various characterization methods were used, including infrared vibrational spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, and SEM. In addition to morphology, porosity, permeability, and mechanical strength, a wide range of properties of CDs-Ag NPs were studied. Increasing CDs-Ag NPs improves membrane strength and selectiveness (reduced pore size). At the same time, pore size and permeability are reduced.

Despite this, adding more NPs than the optimal amount decreases the mechanical strength of the material as well as increases porosity and permeability. Following the characterization of the CDs-Ag NPs incorporated into the polycarbonate membrane, tests to assess membrane biofouling, antibacterial activity, and membrane filtration revealed that the membrane is capable of being functionalized. The future of wastewater treatment may be improved by polycarbonate copolymer membranes containing CDs-Ag NPs.

Increasing pressure or flow rate does not improve oil rejection. Performance comparisons between PC membranes containing CDs-Ag NPs and PC membranes without these NPs show that these NPs also affect PC membrane flux and performance. Synthetic samples were purified at different concentrations using the optimized membrane. Emulsions containing high levels of oil should be pre-treated with ion exchange resins or modified activated carbon.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been supported by the project Sustainable Process Integration Laboratory – SPIL, funded as project No. CZ.02.1.01/0.0/0.0/15_003/0000456, the Operational Programme Research, Development and Education of the Czech Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports by EU European Structural and Investment Funds, Operational Programme Research, Development and Education.
